Behind the Paycheck: Salary Patterns in DC Public Employment¶

This project analyzes the DC Public Employee Salary dataset, which contains salary and employment information for public employees in Washington, DC. The dataset includes details such as employee job title, agency or department description, grade, compensation rate, hire date, appointment type, and object ID.

This dataset is analytically interesting because public salary data can reveal patterns in government employment, compensation differences across agencies, and the types of positions associated with higher compensation rates. By studying these patterns, this project explores how employee role, agency, grade, appointment type, and hire date may relate to compensation rate differences in DC public employment.

Objective and Research Question¶

The objective of this project is to examine compensation patterns among Washington, DC public employees and identify which factors appear to be most associated with higher compensation rates. The main research question is:

Which features appear to be most strongly associated with higher public employee compensation in Washington, DC?

Additional research questions:

  1. Which agencies have the highest average compensation?
  2. Which job titles are associated with the highest compensation?
  3. How is compensation distributed across DC public employees?
  4. Does employee grade appear to relate to compensation?
  5. Are there noticeable outliers or unusually high compensations in the dataset?
  6. Does hire date or length of service reveal any compensation patterns?
In [1]:
#importing libraries
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns
import plotly.express as px
In [2]:
#loads in the dataset
df = pd.read_csv("DC_Public_Employee_Salary.csv")

The initial data understanding stage shows the structure of the dataset, including the number of rows and columns, the column names, the different data types, the missing values, and the summary statistics. This step helps identify which variables are useful for compensation rate analysis and which columns may need to be cleaned before visualizing them.

Data Understanding and Cleaning¶

In [12]:
#creates a copy of the original dataset
df_clean = df.copy()
In [13]:
#standardizes the column names — strip, lowercase, then rename to readable names
df_clean.columns = df_clean.columns.str.strip().str.lower()
df_clean = df_clean.rename(columns={"jobtitle": "job_title", "descrshort": "agency","hiredate_string": "hire_date","gvt_type_of_appt": "appointment_type"})

#drops columns irrelevant to comprate analysis
df_clean = df_clean.drop(columns=["first_name", "last_name", "objectid"])
print("Columns after rename and drop:", df_clean.columns.tolist())

Columns after rename and drop: ['job_title', 'agency', 'grade', 'comprate', 'hire_date', 'appointment_type']
In [14]:
#standardizes job title capitalization
df_clean["job_title"] = df_clean["job_title"].str.title()
In [15]:
#fills the missing grade values with "Unknown"
df_clean["grade"] = df_clean["grade"].fillna("Unknown")

The grade column had 40 missing values. Since grade is a categorical variable, I filled the missing values with "Unknown" instead of deleting the rows or using a numerical replacement. This allowed me to keep the employee records while still making the missing grade values clear during analysis.

In [16]:
#removes the duplicate rows
df_clean = df_clean.drop_duplicates()
In [17]:
#removes extra spaces from text columns
df_clean = df_clean.apply(lambda col: col.str.strip() if col.dtype == "object" else col)
In [18]:
#converts the hire date into datetime format
df_clean["hire_date"] = pd.to_datetime(df_clean["hire_date"], errors="coerce")
In [19]:
print(df_clean.columns.tolist())

['job_title', 'agency', 'grade', 'comprate', 'hire_date', 'appointment_type']
In [20]:
#displays the cleaned data
df_clean.head()

#checks the shape after cleaning
print("Original shape:", df.shape)
print("Cleaned shape:", df_clean.shape)

Original shape: (40611, 9)
Cleaned shape: (33563, 6)

I created a cleaned copy of the original dataset so the raw data would remain unchanged. I standardized the column names, removed duplicate rows, removed the extra spaces from text fields, renamed columns for easier understanding, and converted the hire date column into a datetime format. These steps make the dataset easier to analyze and reduce errors when grouping, filtering, and visualizing the data.

Exploratory Data Analysis¶

This section explores the dataset before creating the main visualizations. The goal is to understand compensation rate ranges, common job titles, agency representation, and the group-level compensation rate patterns.

Data Visualization and Interpretation¶

Visualization 1: Distribution of Compensation Rates¶

Intent: This visualization shows how compensation rates are distributed across DC public employees.

In [32]:
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
ax = sns.histplot(data=df_clean, x="comprate", bins=30, kde=True, label="Compensation Rate")
plt.title("Distribution of DC Public Employee Compensation Rates")
plt.xlabel("Compensation Rate (USD)")
plt.ylabel("Number of Employees")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.axvline(df_clean["comprate"].median(), color="red", linestyle="--", label=f'Median: ${df_clean["comprate"].median():,.0f}')
plt.axvline(df_clean["comprate"].mean(), color="lime", linestyle="--", label=f'Mean: ${df_clean["comprate"].mean():,.0f}')
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This graph shows the overall spread of compensation rates in the dataset. If the distribution is right-skewed, that means most employees earn within a lower-to-middle range while a smaller number of employees have much higher compensation rates.

Visualization 2: Top Agencies by Number of Employees¶

Intent: This visualization identifies which agencies appear most often in the dataset.

In [33]:
top_agency_counts = df_clean["agency"].value_counts().head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(x=top_agency_counts.values, y=top_agency_counts.index)
plt.title("Top 10 Agencies by Number of Employees")
plt.xlabel("Number of Employees")
plt.ylabel("Agency")
plt.show()

This chart shows which agencies have the largest number of employee records. This matters because larger agencies may have more influence on the overall compensation rate distribution.

Visualization 3: Top Agencies by Average Compensation Rate¶

Intent: This visualization compares agencies based on average compensation rate.

In [34]:
top_agency_comprate = df_clean.groupby("agency")["comprate"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(x=top_agency_comprate.values, y=top_agency_comprate.index, color="pink")
plt.title("Top 10 Agencies by Average Compensation Rate (USD)")
plt.xlabel("Average Compensation Rate (USD)")   # typo fixed
plt.ylabel("Agency")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This chart shows which agencies have the highest average compensation rates. This helps answer the research question by showing whether agency type appears connected to compensation rate differences.

Visualization 4: Top Job Titles by Average Compensation Rate¶

Intent: This visualization identifies the job titles associated with the highest average compensation rates.

In [35]:
top_job_comprate = df_clean.groupby("job_title")["comprate"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False).head(10)
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.barplot(x=top_job_comprate.index, y=top_job_comprate.values, color="red")
plt.title("Top 10 Job Titles by Average Compensation Rate")
plt.xlabel("Job Title")
plt.ylabel("Average Compensation Rate")
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.show()

This chart shows how compensation rate differs by job title. Higher-paying job titles may reflect specialized, senior, technical, or leadership roles within DC government.

Visualization 5: Average Compensation Rate by Appointment Type¶

Intent: This visualization compares compensation rate across different appointment types.

In [36]:
appt_comprate = df_clean.groupby("appointment_type")["comprate"].mean().sort_values(ascending=False)

plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.barplot(x=appt_comprate.index, y=appt_comprate.values, color="green")
plt.title("Average Compensation Rate by Appointment Type")
plt.xlabel("Appointment Type")
plt.ylabel("Average Compensation Rate")
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.show()

This chart shows whether compensation rate differs across appointment categories. If certain appointment types have higher average compensation rates, that may suggest differences in job security, role classification, or employee status.

Visualization 6: Compensation Rate Spread Across Top Agencies¶

Intent: This boxplot shows compensation rate variation within the agencies that appear most often in the dataset.

In [37]:
top_10_agencies = df_clean["agency"].value_counts().head(10).index
top_agency_df = df_clean[df_clean["agency"].isin(top_10_agencies)]

plt.figure(figsize=(14, 7))
sns.boxplot(data=top_agency_df, x="agency", y="comprate", color="darkorange")
plt.title("Compensation Rate Distribution Across Top 10 Agencies by Employee Count")
plt.xlabel("Agency")
plt.ylabel("Compensation Rate")
plt.xticks(rotation=45, ha="right")
plt.show()

This boxplot shows the spread of compensation rates within major agencies. It helps reveal whether some agencies have wider compensation rates ranges, more outliers, or higher median compensation rates than others.

Visualization 7: Average Compensation Rate by Hire Year¶

Intent: This visualization explores whether hire year is related to compensation rate patterns.

In [38]:
comprate_by_year = df_clean.groupby("hire_year")["comprate"].mean().dropna()

plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.lineplot(x=comprate_by_year.index, y=comprate_by_year.values, marker="o", color="crimson")
plt.title("Average Compensation Rate by Hire Year")
plt.xlabel("Hire Year")
plt.ylabel("Average Compensation Rate")
plt.xticks(rotation=45)
plt.show()

This line graph shows how average compensation rate varies by hire year. It can help identify whether employees hired in certain time periods tend to have higher or lower average compensation rates.

Visualization 8: Compensation Rate by Years of Service¶

Intent: This scatterplot explores whether employees with more years of service tend to have higher compensation rates.

In [39]:
#creates years of service based on hire date
current_year = 2026
df_clean["years_of_service"] = current_year - df_clean["hire_date"].dt.year
plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
sns.scatterplot(data=df_clean, x="years_of_service", y="comprate", alpha=0.5, color="red")
plt.title("Compensation Rate by Years of Service")
plt.xlabel("Years of Service")
plt.ylabel("Compensation Rate")
plt.show()

This scatterplot shows the relationship between years of service and compensation rate. If higher compensation rates appear more often among employees with more years of service, that may suggest that seniority plays some role in pay. However, if the points are widely spread out, then years of service alone does not fully explain differences in compensation rates.

Visualization 9: Correlation Heatmap¶

Intent: This heatmap compares the relationships between numeric variables in the dataset.

In [40]:
#converts grade to numeric when possible
df_clean["grade_numeric"] = pd.to_numeric(df_clean["grade"], errors="coerce")
#selects numeric columns for the heatmap
numeric_cols = ["comprate", "years_of_service", "grade_numeric"]

#creates correlation matrix
corr_matrix = df_clean[numeric_cols].corr()

plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
sns.heatmap(corr_matrix, annot=True, cmap="Reds", linewidths=0.5)

plt.title("Correlation Heatmap of Compensation, Years of Service, and Grade")
plt.show()

This heatmap shows how strongly compensation rate, years of service, and grade relate to each other. Values closer to 1 indicate a stronger positive relationship, while values closer to 0 suggest a weaker relationship. This helps show whether compensation rate is more connected to grade, seniority, or neither.

Visualization 10: Interactive Average Compensation Rate by Agency (Plotly)¶

Intent: This interactive bar chart lets the viewer hover over each agency to see exact average compensation rate and employee count which is a detail that static charts cannot provide.

In [41]:
#top 15 agencies by average comprate for a richer interactive view
top15_agency = df_clean.groupby("agency")["comprate"].agg(["mean", "count"]).reset_index()
top15_agency.columns = ["agency", "avg_comprate", "employee_count"]
top15_agency = top15_agency.sort_values("avg_comprate", ascending=False).head(15)
fig = px.bar(top15_agency, x="avg_comprate", y="agency",orientation="h", color="avg_comprate", color_continuous_scale="Oranges", hover_data={"employee_count": True, "avg_comprate": ":$,.0f"},labels={"avg_comprate": "Avg Compensation Rate (USD)", "agency": "Agency", "employee_count": "# Employees"},title="Top 15 Agencies by Average Compensation Rate (Interactive)")
fig.update_layout(yaxis={"categoryorder": "total ascending"}, coloraxis_showscale=False)
fig.show()

This interactive chart confirms that smaller, specialized agencies (CJDT, MOLC, OSA) have the highest average compensation rates, while large agencies like DCPS employ many more people at a lower average compensation rate. Hovering over each bar reveals the exact average compensation rate and employee count, showing the tradeoff between agency size and average compensation rate.

Visualization 11: Interactive Compensation Rate Distribution by Appointment Type¶

Intent: This interactive box plot shows how compensation rates vary by appointment type. It helps compare the spread, middle values, and outliers, while also letting you hover over points to see details like job title.

In [52]:
#orders the appointment types by median comprate
appt_order = df_clean.groupby("appointment_type")["comprate"].median().sort_values(ascending=False).index.tolist()

fig2 = px.box(df_clean, x="appointment_type", y="comprate", color="appointment_type", category_orders={"appointment_type": appt_order},labels={"comprate": "Compensation Rate (USD)", "appointment_type": "Appointment Type"},title="Compensation Rate Distribution by Appointment Type",hover_data=["job_title", "agency"])
fig2.update_layout(showlegend=False, xaxis_tickangle=-45)
fig2.show()

This interactive box plot reveals the full compensation rate range within each appointment type, not just the average. Executive Regular (Exe - Reg) appointments show both the highest median and the widest spread, confirming they represent senior leadership roles. Hovering over individual points reveals specific job titles and agencies, showing which roles drive the high-end outliers within each category.

Visualization 12: Surprise Plot Compensation Rate Inequality Within Agencies¶

Intent: This plot compares the average compensation rate of the top 10% and bottom 10% of earners within each of the 10 largest agencies, revealing which agencies have the most extreme internal pay gaps which is an insight that average compensation rate alone conceals.

In [43]:
#top 10% vs bottom 10% compensation rate gap by agency
top10_ag = df_clean["agency"].value_counts().head(10).index
agency_df = df_clean[df_clean["agency"].isin(top10_ag)]
In [44]:
#calculates the average compensation rate for top and bottom 10% in each agency
def top10_mean(x):
    return x[x >= x.quantile(0.90)].mean()

def bot10_mean(x):
    return x[x <= x.quantile(0.10)].mean()
In [45]:
gap_df = agency_df.groupby("agency")["comprate"].agg(top_10=top10_mean,bottom_10=bot10_mean).reset_index()
gap_df["gap"] = gap_df["top_10"] - gap_df["bottom_10"]
gap_df = gap_df.sort_values("gap", ascending=True)

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(12, 7))

ax.barh(gap_df["agency"], gap_df["top_10"], color="green", alpha=0.85, label="Top 10% avg compensation rate")
ax.barh(gap_df["agency"], gap_df["bottom_10"], color="navy", alpha=0.85, label="Bottom 10% avg compensation rate")

#annotates the gap
for i, (_, row) in enumerate(gap_df.iterrows()):
    ax.text(row["top_10"] + 1000, i, f'Gap: ${row["gap"]:,.0f}', va="center", fontsize=9, color="black")

ax.set_xlabel("Compensation Rate (USD)")
ax.set_ylabel("Agency")
ax.set_title("Surprise Plot: Compensation Rate Inequality Within DC's Largest Agencies\n(Top 10% vs Bottom 10% Average Compensation Rate)")
ax.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

This plot shows something that average compensation charts can easily miss: compensation rate differences within agencies vary across the dataset. Some agencies may have a moderate average compensation rate overall, but still show a large gap between their highest- and lowest-paid employees. This likely reflects the mix of positions within each agency, where senior specialists, managers, and entry-level employees may all be grouped under the same department. Because of this, agency alone does not fully explain compensation. The specific role or level of responsibility within the agency is just as important.

Additional Analysis / Findings¶

Additional Analysis 1: Compensation Rate Outliers¶

Motivation: I wanted to identify unusually high compensation rates because outliers can affect the average compensation rate and reveal specialized or senior-level roles.

In [46]:
#identifies comp rate outliers using the IQR method
Q1 = df_clean["comprate"].quantile(0.25)
Q3 = df_clean["comprate"].quantile(0.75)
IQR = Q3 - Q1
upper_bound = Q3 + 1.5 * IQR

#defines comprate_outliers and employees above the upper bound
comprate_outliers = df_clean[df_clean["comprate"] > upper_bound]

print("Upper bound for compensation rate outliers:", upper_bound)
print("Number of compensation rate outliers:", comprate_outliers.shape[0])

comprate_outliers[["job_title", "agency", "comprate"]].sort_values(by="comprate", ascending=False).head(10)

In [47]:
plt.figure(figsize=(12, 6))
sns.boxplot(data=df_clean, x="comprate")
plt.title("Compensation Rate Outliers Among DC Public Employees")
plt.xlabel("Compensation Rate")
plt.show()

This boxplot shows the overall spread of compensation rates in the dataset, making it easy to identify where outliers fall relative to the bulk of employees.

Additional Analysis 2: Compensation Rate Range Categories¶

Motivation: I wanted to group employees into compensation rate ranges to make the compensation rate distribution easier to interpret.

In [48]:
#creates comprate range categories
df_clean["comprate_range"] = pd.cut(df_clean["comprate"], bins=[0, 50000, 75000, 100000, 125000, 150000, df_clean["comprate"].max()], labels=["Under 50K", "50K-75K", "75K-100K", "100K-125K", "125K-150K", "150K+"])
df_clean["comprate_range"].value_counts().sort_index()
Out[48]:
comprate_range
Under 50K    5701
50K-75K      5156
75K-100K     7449
100K-125K    7795
125K-150K    4911
150K+        2551
Name: count, dtype: int64
In [49]:
comprate_range_counts = df_clean["comprate_range"].value_counts().sort_index()
colors =["deepskyblue", "dodgerblue", "mediumslateblue", "blueviolet", "mediumorchid", "skyblue"]
explode = [0.05] * len(comprate_range_counts)
plt.figure(figsize=(9, 9))
plt.pie(comprate_range_counts.values, labels=comprate_range_counts.index, autopct="%1.2f%%", startangle=90, colors=colors[:len(comprate_range_counts)], explode=explode, shadow=True)
plt.title("Share of Employees by Compensation Rate Range")
plt.show()

This pie chart shows the share of employees in each compensation rate range. It makes it easier to compare how much of the dataset falls into each compensation bracket. Larger slices represent compensation ranges with more employees, while smaller slices show less common compensation ranges.

Insights and Conclusion¶

This project examined compensation rate patterns among DC public employees to understand which features appear most associated with higher compensation rates. The analysis suggests that compensation rate differences are connected to agency, job title, appointment type, and possibly hire year. Agencies and job titles showed especially clear differences in average compensation rate, which indicates that the type of work and department structure may play an important role in compensation.

The research question was answered by comparing compensation rates across several job-related features. The most significant patterns came from average compensation rate by agency, average compensation rate by job title, compensation rate distribution, and compensation rate outlier analysis. The visualizations showed that compensation rates are not evenly distributed, and some roles or agencies appear to be connected to much higher compensation rates than others.

One limitation of this analysis is that the dataset does not fully explain why compensation rate differences exist. It does not include every possible factor, such as education level, years of experience before government employment, performance history, or exact job responsibilities. Future analysis could combine this compensation rate data with more detailed employment classification data to better understand what drives public sector compensation.

In [50]:
#exports the cleaned data
df_clean.to_csv("chiebuka_onwuka_final_project.csv", index=False)
